# CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINE

# Diagnosis of Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis in Adults, 2020 Clinical Practice Guideline: Summary for Clinicians

Megan A. Koster1, Carey C. Thomson1, Bridget F. Collins2, Alex R. Jenkins3, Joseph K. Ruminjo4, and Ganesh Raghu2

1Division of Pulmonary and Critical Care, Department of Medicine, Mount Auburn Hospital, Harvard Medical School, Cambridge, Massachusetts; 2Center for Interstitial Lung Diseases, Department of Medicine, University of Washington Medical Center, Seattle, Washington; 3Division of Respiratory Medicine, University of Nottingham, Nottingham, United Kingdom; and 4American Thoracic Society, New York, New York

Summary of: Raghu G, Remy-Jardin M, Ryerson CJ, Myers JL, Kreuter M, Vasakova M, et al. Diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis in adults: an official ATS/JRS/ALAT clinical practice guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2020;202:e36–e69.

Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP) is characterized by inflammatory and/or fibrotic destruction of the lung parenchyma and small airways mediated by an immune response to an inhaled antigen in a susceptible individual1, 2. Historic categories of acute, subacute, or chronic HP without precise radiographic, histopathologic, and prognostic correlates have made diagnosis and treatment challenging. An ad hoc, multidisciplinary committee of international experts from North America, Japan, Latin America, Europe, and Australia, appointed by the American Thoracic Society, Japanese Respiratory Society, and the Asociación Latinoamericana del Tórax, consolidated evidence informing clinical features of HP, publishing new diagnostic criteria and graded recommendations for the diagnostic approach2. This summary is intended to provide the practicing clinician with key points from the guideline.

This guideline complements the 2018 American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society/Japanese Respiratory Society/Asociación Latinoamericana del Tórax guideline on the diagnosis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF) and focuses on the diagnostic approach to the adult patient with interstitial lung disease (ILD) in whom the history suggests a potential culprit exposure3.

# Clinical Manifestations

NFHP and FHP are characterized by symptoms of dyspnea, cough, or, less frequently, constitutional symptoms such as weight loss, chills, low-grade fever, malaise, chest tightness, and wheezing1. Symptoms can develop acutely over days to weeks or insidiously over months to years. Timing of symptom onset has not been associated with presence or absence of fibrosis. When present, inspiratory squeaks are characteristic findings on auscultation. Patients may stabilize or fully recover (especially in NFHP) upon elimination of the relevant exposure or progress to respiratory failure and death. Factors associated with poor prognosis include patients with FHP (particularly with a usual interstitial pneumonitis [UIP] pattern), cigarette smoking, lower baseline vital capacity (VC), lack of bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) lymphocytosis, persistent exposure to the inciting agent, and lack of identification of the inciting agent6, 7.

# Definition

HP is the culmination of a dysregulated immune response to an inciting inhaled antigen when identified in a sensitized susceptible individual manifesting as inflammation and/or fibrosis of the lung parenchyma and small airways2. Previously described categories of acute, subacute, and chronic HP are arbitrary and fail to meaningfully inform expectations of prognosis. Conversely, radiographic and histopathologic categorization are strong determinants of prognosis4, 5. The predominant presence or absence of radiographic or histopathologic fibrosis can be leveraged to define a new classification of nonfibrotic HP (NFHP) purely inflammatory phenotype versus fibrotic HP (FHP) that includes mixed inflammatory plus fibrotic or purely fibrotic phenotypes.

# Epidemiology

HP prevalence varies with regional differences in climate, occupational exposures, and environmental exposures and is highest among older individuals in the fifth and sixth decade of life8. Patients with FHP are more likely to be older, more unlikely to have an identified inciting agent, and more likely to have lower VC and diffusion capacity than patients with NFHP9.

From the GRADE (Grading of Recommendations, Assessment, and Evaluation) working group. Adapted by permission from Reference 2.

*The meaning of a suggestion is the same as a weak or conditional recommendation in typical GRADE nomenclature.

# Pathogenesis

Although the culprit exposure is frequently not identified, HP develops in response to repeated exposure to one or more inciting agents, which may take the form of organic particulates (including microbial, animal, or plant antigens) or inorganic chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and metals. Antigens known to cause HP are listed in the guideline (2). The ensuing immune response includes both humoral (i.e., antigen-specific immunoglobulin [Ig]G antibodies) and cellular (i.e., T-helper cell type 1) components, which result in lymphocyte-predominant and granulomatous inflammation (10). Genetic predisposition is the best-studied host susceptibility factor, including impairments in innate and adaptive immunity, telomere-related mutations, and, in patients with FHP, polymorphisms in MUC5B (mucin 5b) promoter (11–13).

# Fibrotic HP

FHP is suggested by concomitant fibrosis and bronchiolar obstruction. In Typical FHP, lung fibrosis is prominent in mid/lower lung zones or equally distributed with relative basilar sparing without central or peripheral predominance. Evidence of bronchiolar obstruction includes ill-defined centrilobular nodules, GGO, mosaic attenuation, air trapping, and/or the three-density pattern, which is highly specific for FHP (14). HRCT features Compatible with FHP include variants in fibrosis pattern (UIP or extensive GGO with superimposed fibrosis) and distribution (upper lobe, peribronchovascular, or subpleural predominance) with corresponding small airway disease. Fibrosis alone (UIP, nonspecific interstitial pneumonitis [NSIP], or organizing pneumonia pattern), without coexistent bronchiolar obstruction, is Indeterminate for FHP.

# Radiological Features

Volumetric scanning of the chest by high-resolution computed tomographic (HRCT) imaging with two series of supine images (one at deep inspiration and another after prolonged expiration) is the preferred diagnostic approach for HP. Prone or contrast-enhanced images are not required. The guideline proposes a classification scheme for HRCT patterns associated with attenuation and at least one abnormality suggesting small airway disease (ill-defined <5 mm centrilobular nodules on inspiratory images or air trapping on expiratory images). In NFHP, mosaic attenuation usually reflects lobules with pneumonitis (high attenuation) adjacent to lobules with normal or decreased attenuation due to bronchiolar obstruction. In the appropriate clinical context, features suggestive of HP are present in both NFHP and FHP, the latter having the additional component of.

"For policymakers","The recommendation can be adapted as policy in most situations including for the use as performance indicators. Policymaking will require substantial debates and involvement of many stakeholders. Policies are also more likely to vary between regions. Performance indicators would have to focus on the fact that adequate deliberation about the management options has taken place."

items.md: # Pathogenesis
items.value: Pathogenesis

items.md: Although the culprit exposure is frequently not identified, HP develops in response to repeated exposure to one or more inciting agents, which may take the form of organic particulates (including microbial, animal, or plant antigens) or inorganic chemicals, pharmaceuticals, and metals. Antigens known to cause HP are listed in the guideline (2). The ensuing immune response includes both humoral (i.e., antigen-specific immunoglobulin [Ig]G antibodies) and cellular (i.e., T-helper cell type 1) components, which result in lymphocyte-predominant and granulomatous inflammation (10). Genetic predisposition is the best-studied host susceptibility factor, including impairments in innate and adaptive immunity, telomere-related mutations, and, in patients with FHP, polymorphisms in MUC5B (mucin 5b) promoter (11–13).

items.md: # Fibrotic HP
items.value: Fibrotic HP

items.md: FHP is suggested by concomitant fibrosis and bronchiolar obstruction. In Typical FHP, lung fibrosis is prominent in mid/lower lung zones or equally distributed with relative basilar sparing without central or peripheral predominance. Evidence of bronchiolar obstruction includes ill-defined centrilobular nodules, GGO, mosaic attenuation, air trapping, and/or the three-density pattern, which is highly specific for FHP (14). HRCT features Compatible with FHP include variants in fibrosis pattern (UIP or extensive GGO with superimposed fibrosis) and distribution (upper lobe, peribronchovascular, or subpleural predominance) with corresponding small airway disease. Fibrosis alone (UIP, nonspecific interstitial pneumonitis [NSIP], or organizing pneumonia pattern), without coexistent bronchiolar obstruction, is Indeterminate for FHP.

items.md: # Radiological Features
items.value: Radiological Features

items.md: Volumetric scanning of the chest by high-resolution computed tomographic (HRCT) imaging with two series of supine images (one at deep inspiration and another after prolonged expiration) is the preferred diagnostic approach for HP. Prone or contrast-enhanced images are not required. The guideline proposes a classification scheme for HRCT patterns associated with attenuation and at least one abnormality suggesting small airway disease (ill-defined <5 mm centrilobular nodules on inspiratory images or air trapping on expiratory images). In NFHP, mosaic attenuation usually reflects lobules with pneumonitis (high attenuation) adjacent to lobules with normal or decreased attenuation due to bronchiolar obstruction. In the appropriate clinical context, features suggestive of HP are present in both NFHP and FHP, the latter having the additional component of.

pages.md:
# CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINE

Probable NFHP requires lymphocyte-rich, bronchiolocentric interstitial pneumonia and associated bronchiolitis in the absence of granulomatous inflammation. Indeterminate NFHP lacks granulomatous inflammation and features cellular bronchiolocentric interstitial pneumonia or unexplained cellular bronchiolitis.

Distinguishing FHP from other fibrotic idiopathic interstitial pneumonias often requires biopsy sampling from more than one site, similar to IPF, in which diagnostic discordance between sites is not uncommon. For FHP, typical histopathological features include chronic fibrosing interstitial pneumonia or airway centered fibrosis and poorly formed nonnecrotizing granulomas without features in any biopsy site to suggest alternative diagnosis. Features of Probable FHP are similar to FHP but lack granulomas. Indeterminate for FHP (MDD). Identifying a culprit exposure is not essential to diagnosis; up to 50% of patients with FHP do not have an identifiable exposure even when evaluated at an ILD center.

Patients with NFHP often have onset of pulmonary and systemic symptoms temporally related to an acute and identifiable exposure with centrilobular nodularity seen on HRCT imaging and BAL lymphocytosis. In contrast, patients with FHP are less likely to have an identifiable antigen exposure and more likely to have insidious onset of pulmonary symptoms over time and a nonspecific BAL cell differential with fibrotic changes on HRCT.

# Diagnostic Criteria

No individual feature on history, HRCT imaging, or histopathology is adequate in isolation or mandatory to make a diagnosis of HP. Diagnosis is ideally made via multidisciplinary discussion.

pages.md:
CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINE

inhalational challenge); 2) imaging pattern; and 3) BAL lymphocytosis and/or histopathologic findings. Multiple combinations of abnormalities can result in HP diagnosis (Figure 2). The level of diagnostic confidence follows an approach proposed for fibrotic ILDs: deﬁnite (>90% confidence), high confidence (80–89%), moderate confidence (70–79%), and low confidence (<69%) (17). HP diagnosis of the highest confidence can be made in patients with a culprit exposure, typical HP HRCT pattern, and BAL lymphocytosis without invasive testing. For all other patients, MDD with or without subsequent histopathological sampling is recommended to achieve an acceptable level of diagnostic confidence.

In the absence of exposure history and the presence of HRCT image pattern indeterminate for HP with histopathology typical for HP, the confidence level of the diagnosis of HP may be increased to “definite” if the pathologist’s conclusion of typical HP persists after reevaluation in the context of additional clinical information or an expert second opinion on histopathology (Figure 2).

# Questionnaires

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes HP, the panel made no recommendation or suggestion regarding the use of a specific questionnaire to identify potential inciting antigens of HP (very low quality of evidence). The panel acknowledged the lack of standardization for serum IgG testing worldwide and suggested development and validation of a standardized hypersensitivity panel (2, 18).

# BAL Fluid Lymphocyte Cellular Analysis

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes NFHP, the panel recommended BAL with lymphocyte cellular analysis (strong recommendation, very low quality of evidence). For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes FHP, the panel suggested performing BAL with lymphocyte cellular analysis (very low quality of evidence). Based on the available evidence, most of the panel felt that BAL fluid cellular lymphocyte analysis can be key in distinguishing NFHP from sarcoidosis and FHP from IPF and sarcoidosis. The panel could not identify a threshold.

# Serum IgG Testing

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes NFHP or FHP, the panel suggested performing serum IgG testing. Most of the panel agreed that serum IgG testing was convenient and generated adequate supportive data for HP diagnosis, but alone would be insufficient to confirm or exclude HP diagnosis. The panel based its decision on a lack of available evidence but agreed as to the potential role of a questionnaire as part of the diagnostic pathway. Future research should focus on the development and validation of a comprehensive questionnaire. In the interim, a thorough history is recommended to identify potential causative antigens (2, 18).

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes FHP, the panel made no recommendation or suggestion regarding TBBx (very low quality of evidence).

# Transbronchial Forceps Lung Biopsy

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes NFHP, the panel suggested performing transbronchial forceps lung biopsy.

The panel agreed that diagnostic yield (number of procedures yielding a histopathological diagnosis out of total procedures performed) is important.

rows: Q1. Should patients with newly detected ILD in chest radiographs or a CT scan of the chest, with or without an overt history of exposures capable of causing ILD in the patient’s environment at home, work, or elsewhere, be subjected to formal questioning using a questionnaire to raise the possibility that 1) potential inciting agent/s of HP are the etiology of the ILD and 2) the diagnosis of the ILD is HP?

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes fibrotic or nonfibrotic HP, the panel makes no recommendation or suggestion for or against the use of a specific questionnaire to identify potential inciting agents of HP (very low quality of evidence). The panel recommends the development and validation of a questionnaire.

Q2. Should patients with newly detected ILD in chest radiographs or a CT scan of the chest, with or without an overt history of exposures capable of causing ILD in the patient’s environment at home, work, or elsewhere, undergo serum testing for IgG antibodies against specific antigens to raise the possibility that 1) potential inciting agent/s of HP are the etiology of the ILD and 2) the diagnosis of the ILD is HP?

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes fibrotic or nonfibrotic HP, the panel suggests performing serum IgG testing that targets potential antigens associated with HP (very low quality of evidence).

Q3. Should patients with newly detected ILD in chest radiographs or a CT scan of the chest, with or without a history of exposure capable of causing HP, undergo bronchoalveolar lavage fluid lymphocyte cellular analysis to diagnose HP?

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes nonfibrotic HP, the panel recommends bronchoalveolar lavage with lymphocyte cellular analysis (very low quality of evidence). For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes fibrotic HP, the panel suggests performing bronchoalveolar lavage with lymphocyte cellular analysis (very low quality of evidence).

Q4. Should patients with newly detected ILD in chest radiographs or a CT scan of the chest, with or without a history of exposure capable of causing HP, undergo transbronchial forceps lung biopsy to diagnose HP?

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes nonfibrotic HP, the panel suggests performing transbronchial forceps lung biopsy (very low quality of evidence). For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes fibrotic HP, the panel makes no recommendation or suggestion for or against performing transbronchial forceps lung biopsy (very low quality of evidence).

Q5. Should patients with newly detected ILD in chest radiographs or a CT scan of the chest, with or without a history of exposure capable of causing HP, undergo transbronchial lung cryobiopsy to diagnose HP?

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes nonfibrotic HP, the panel makes no recommendation or suggestion for or against performing transbronchial cryobiopsy (very low quality of evidence). For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes fibrotic HP, the panel suggests performing transbronchial cryobiopsy (very low quality of evidence).
Q6. Should patients with newly detected ILD in chest radiographs or CT scan chest, with or without a history of exposure capable of causing HP, undergo surgical lung biopsy to diagnose HP?

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes fibrotic or nonfibrotic HP, the panel suggests performing surgical lung biopsy after alternative diagnostic options have been exhausted (very low quality of evidence).

CLINICAL PRACTICE GUIDELINE

procedures performed) of TBBx was or suspected HP (100%), ILD (7.2%), failure (2%), and bleeding (1%). The panel agreed that diagnostic yield by SLB was high with a low complication rate and suggested SLB should be used after comprehensive MDD when other diagnostic tests fail to result in a definitive diagnosis (2).

Complications with TBBx include pneumothorax (7%) and moderate to severe bleeding (4%) (2, 21).

# Transbronchial Lung Cryobiopsy

For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes NFHP, the panel made no recommendation or suggestion regarding transbronchial lung cryobiopsy (TBLC) (very low quality of evidence). For patients with newly detected ILD whose differential diagnosis includes FHP, the panel suggested performing TBLC (very low quality of evidence).

Pooled data showed that the diagnostic yields were 91%, 82%, and 82% among patients with known or suspected HP, ILD, and DLD, respectively. Patients with known or suspected HP (91%), ILD (9%), or DLD (9%) had HP confirmed when a diagnosis was made by SLB. Complications with SLB include persistent pneumothorax after chest tube removal (4%), prolonged air leak (3%), respiratory infection (3%), delayed wound healing (3%), procedural mortality (2%), postprocedural exacerbation or respiratory.

# Future Directions

This guideline defines clinical manifestations, pathogenesis, and radiological and histopathological features of HP and proposes new diagnostic criteria and graded recommendations for the diagnostic approach. Further study of HP pathophysiology and natural history and evaluation of diagnostic and therapeutic approaches is crucial. Future research should aim to validate and standardize a questionnaire for clinical use, determine a BAL lymphocytosis threshold, identify specific HP antibodies, and explore biomarkers of disease. There is a need for clinical and translational research, including HP-specific registries, to explore management of HP by subtype. Updating of this guideline is warranted upon the availability of new evidence. ■

Author disclosures are available with the text of this article at www.atsjournals.org.
